coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and their interactions with recognition molecules for enhanced nucleic acid detection
coredata.dc:creator: Garg, Rishabh
Prasad, Dinesh
coredata.dc:description: Carbon Dots (C-dots) have exceptional fluorescence and incident wavelength alteration capabilities because of their π-π* electron transitions between the surface-trapped charges. They have clear, considerate and cost-effective applications in the domain of bio-sensing, optical imaging, medical diagnostics, fluorescence chemotherapy, forensics, and environmentology. Advances in the production process of C-dots can change their optical and chemical characteristics, allowing them to interact with a variety of chemicals and ions that can be exploited for the DNA detection in point-of-care devices. In the current scenario of pathogenic disease prevention, metagenomics and industrial processes, alternative genetic material identification is critical. This review focuses on the existing carbon dots-based DNA detection technologies and their interactions with other components such as metallic salts, dyes, and biological chemicals based on their surface charge distribution (positive or negative) employed in the DNA diagnostic devices and biosensors with their operating mechanism regarding their target component. These intriguing scientific discoveries and technologies will be extensively examined to translate them into real-world solutions which will have a significant societal and economic impact on overall well-being and innovation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots based Nucleic Acid biosensing techniques using implementable tools.

Green CDs as sensing agent; binding of metal ions on CDs causes fluorescence quenching. Mechanism of fluorescence quenching: (a) CDs donate photo-excited electrons to the vacant d-orbital of metal ions; (b) Energy emitted by CDs is re-absorbed by metal ions; (c) CDs donate electrons to the conduction band of metal ions; (d) Energy transfer from excited-state CDs to metal ions (FRET); (e) CD aggregates with similar CDs transfer an electron to the ground state of that CD (AIEQ); (f) CDs donate their electrons to the ground state of metal ions (SEQ) reprinted from Ref. [92] with the permission from Elsevier. © 2019 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
CDs aided estimation of Cu NCs formation on DNA template with and without clustered Abasic sites; reprinted from Ref. [96] with the permission from Elsevier. © 2017 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
Schematic illustration of the detection steps of DNA target, in the biosensing technique; reprinted from Ref. [98] Copyright © 2018, Springer Nature.
Scheme of the DNA biosensor development; reprinted from Ref. [107] with the permission from Elsevier. © 2017 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

The schematic illustration of dual-emissive optical method for microRNA-21 detection; (A) synthesis and functionalization of B-CDs, (B) synthesis of Y-CDs, and (C) Procedure of microRNA-21 detection; reprinted with the permission from Ref. [121] Copyright © 2022, Springer Nature.
Schematic illustration of the strategy for HPV 16 analysis, reprinted with the permission from Ref. [122] Copyright © 2022 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and their interactions with recognition molecules for enhanced nucleic acid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioengineering and Biotechnology, Birla Institute of Technology, Mesra, Ranchi, Jharkhand, 835215, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Bioengineering and Biotechnology, Birla Institute of Technology, Mesra, Ranchi, Jharkhand, 835215, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Dots (C-dots) have exceptional fluorescence and incident wavelength alteration capabilities because of their π-π* electron transitions between the surface-trapped charges. They have clear, considerate and cost-effective applications in the domain of bio-sensing, optical imaging, medical diagnostics, fluorescence chemotherapy, forensics, and environmentology. Advances in the production process of C-dots can change their optical and chemical characteristics, allowing them to interact with a variety of chemicals and ions that can be exploited for the DNA detection in point-of-care devices. In the current scenario of pathogenic disease prevention, metagenomics and industrial processes, alternative genetic material identification is critical. This review focuses on the existing carbon dots-based DNA detection technologies and their interactions with other components such as metallic salts, dyes, and biological chemicals based on their surface charge distribution (positive or negative) employed in the DNA diagnostic devices and biosensors with their operating mechanism regarding their target component. These intriguing scientific discoveries and technologies will be extensively examined to translate them into real-world solutions which will have a significant societal and economic impact on overall well-being and innovation.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon has been known to humankind since antiquity. Over two hundred years ago, it was discovered in organic compounds, proteins as well as in natural carbon compounds such as its amorphous forms: diamond and graphite. Carbon shows a remarkable ability to form robust covalent bonds with other carbon atoms, or non-metallic elements in its three hybridization states (sp3, sp2 and sp). This allows it to construct a wide variety of materials roaming from small molecules to huge chains [1].

In recent years, a new class of materials has emerged, effectively bridging the gap between organic and native carbon composites. Amidst the landscape of diverse carbon nanostructures, carbon dots (C-dots) have risen as zero or quasi-dimensional nanoparticles, composed of graphitic and turbostratic carbonic species within the 10 nm size range [2,3]. In 2004, Xu et al. discovered C-dots while purifying single-walled carbon nanotube fragments through an arc-discharge process and gel electrophoresis [4]. Distinguished by their composition of sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, C-dots stand apart from other carbon nanostructures like fullerenes, carbon-nonotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds, etc [3]. However, their most remarkable trait is photoluminescence, a phenomenon ignite only when the C-dots undergo passivation through functional groups or ionic species. The origin of their fluorescence lies in the three theories: trapped surface charges, transition of π-π* bond electrons, and the confinement of electron-hole pairs (excitons) at the core, which is known as quantum confinement effect [5–9]. Emitting a broad UV-absorbance spectrum, C-dots typically emit blue to green wavelengths, with some emissions extending into red spectrum [10].

Fuelling the fascination with carbon nanostructures the quest for C-dot synthesis has yielded a plethora of techniques. Li et al., comprehensive report findings about the production, characteristics, and the application of C-dots [11]. This journey unfolds through two major approaches: top-down and bottom-up. The top-down involves breaking down large organic compounds into smaller sizes while the bottom-up strategy crafts C-dots from constituent organic molecules and compounds [12]. Top-down method comprises arc-discharge, laser ablation, or electro-chemical oxidation, offering control over C-dots physical attributes and stability [13]. On the other hand, the bottom-up procedure, integrates hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis, microwave-pyrolysis, combustion and acid treatment to imbue C-dots with desired functional groups and constituents [10,14]. Incorporating the principles of sustainability, C-dots have also been forged from natural resources like fruit juices, seeds, biomass, heralding the era of green synthesis [15–21]. From the seeds of Foeniculum vulgare to Sargassum fluitans, such natural sources have unlocked avenues for cost-effective synthesis [22,23]. The prepared C-dots were applied for the fluorescent tagging of nucleic acids in agarose gel electrophoresis in place of hazardous EtBr [24].

The functional groups woven into C-dots' fabric dictate their properties, with carboxyl and amine groups taking the lead in florescent and solubility. Epoxide, sulfonyl, keto, hydroxyl, and others amplify this symphony, their presence contingent upon the material used for production [25]. [26–31]. Beyond this symphony lies the art of surface passivation, sculpting interactions, surface charges, and fluorescence. Passivation, the process of functionalization, alters luminescence efficiency and solubility, ushering C-dots into organic solvents or aqueous domains, guided by the character of added functional groups [32,33]. The canvas deepens further, as elemental, ionic species, and polymeric forms augment C-dots' surface, ushering in new attributes tailored to specific needs [34]. Surface passivation embraces various techniques including high-temperature acid treatment, plasma treatment, or by the inclusion of a polymer such as poly(ethylene glycol), polyester polymer alloy (PEPA) and silica [35]. The polymeric passivation approach was utilized to create larger-sized polymeric C-dots [36]. Notably, amine rich hydrocarbons stimulates amine-doped C-dots’ surface facilitating their application in detecting explosives and hazardous pollutants such as picric acid [37].

Apart from their photoluminescence, C-dots distinction lies in their non-toxic nature due to the absence of heavy metals, which are generally present in semiconductor materials [38,39]. This quality has propelled their use in biological applications interfacing seamlessly with proteins, nucleic acid and cell [40,41]. From solar cells to catalytic activities and genetic engineering, C-dots showcase their versatility in a myriad of fields [42–49]. Depending upon all the above-mentioned attributes, the C-dots could be broadly categorized into three types, notably: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), and Carbon nanodots (CNDs) [41]. CQDs, often under 5 nm in size, behaving in accordance to quantum physics and quantum effect, possessing unique photophysical, electronic, semi-conducting and optical attributes [50]. GQDs are nanoscale structure that exhibit remarkable chemical and physical stability. They are made up of one or more layers of graphene or graphene oxide (GO) both of which are single-atom-thick sheets of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice. The GQDs are less than 100 nm in diameter and formed when graphene or GO is broken down into extremely small fragments to produce colloidal semiconductor quantum particles (molecules having semiconductor-like optoelectronic characteristics) [51,52]. GQDs have several applications in optoelectronics, solar cells and light-emitting diodes [53–55]. [56]. The CNDs, composed of carbon atoms arranged in honey-comb maze-like structure at the core with multifarious functional groups attached to the outer peripheries or surface [57].

With their potential to revolutionize fields such as bio-sensing, cell imaging, biomarker imaging, electrochemistry, biophysics, and environmental science, C-dots have emerged as promising entities. Their role in genetic material detection is particularly noteworthy, filling the void left by conventional methods [58,59]. A new field of MXene class's material has also been coupled with C-dots to stimulate the accuracy and working of the biosensors, for example, Titanium carbide MXenes functionalized C-dots have been applied for a label-free biosensing of glucose levels in liquid biological samples [60].

Additionally, C-dots have shown promise in identifying ions functioning as co-factors in disorders where enzyme-based reactions fall short [61]. The synergy of C-dots and DNA probes in biosensing devices, founded on alterations in fluorescence intensity upon DNA probe attachment and subsequent recovery during double helix formation, epitomizes the potential of these nanostructures [41,62].

C-dots have emerged as a promising platform for enhancing nucleic acid detection by exploiting their unique interactions with recognition molecules, including dyes, metal derivatives, and DNA probes. These interactions capitalize on the exceptional optical properties of C-dots, which include strong fluorescence and tunable surface chemistry. By functionalizing carbon dots with recognition molecules, researchers can create sensitive and selective biosensing platforms. This article discusses C-dots employed in interaction with various materials for their application in DNA/RNA biosensing devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots in COVID-19 era and interaction with nucleic acid
ce:section.ce:para: For the last few years, severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), responsible for the COVID-19 pandemic [63], and its modified variants have been creating havoc and have caused a heavy loss to the planet. Whether economically or medically [64], the pandemic made us realize the gaps in research and development, including the progress in diagnostics. The COVID-19 pandemic has not been eradicated yet after its outbreak in 2019. Natural sources have always been used as antimicrobials [65], so the synthesis of C-dots from natural sources is gaining prominence in research. As a result, early detection methods and medications that can target coronaviruses are much sought. Cationic C-dots produced by pyrolysis of turmeric or curcumin have been utilized to restrict porcine epidemic diarrhea virus (PEDV), a coronavirus model, and are one of the promising possibilities. By electrostatic contact with the virus, this substance can change the shape of a viral surface protein, blocking the viral entrance and preventing the creation of viral negative-strand RNA [66]. It is fantastic that such easily manufactured C-dots have such assuring anti-viral capabilities. Furthermore, there is a pressing need for a better understanding of the mechanism of C-dots’ anti-viral effects as well as the structural-activity correlation in terms of their shape, size, charge, etc. [67]. Recently, Carbon nanotubes modified by complementary probes for SARS-COVID-19 virus have been used to extract the virus's genetic material in a solution, thereafter can be quantified by performing RT-PCR [68]. Since then the techniques containing C-dots regarding detection of pandemic-causing viruses have been put forward, also discussed further in this review.

To understand the interaction of C-dots with genetic material, Pramanik et al. [69] proposed a method for DNA binding with recognition depending upon the base-pair selectivity. They utilized C-dots made by combusting chicken eggshell membrane which is rich in cysteine, glycine and glycoproteins [70]. In their study, they noticed that the fluorescence of their produced C-dots was more intensified by the thymine (T) and adenine (A) rich DNA strands than the DNA strands rich in cytosine (C) and guanine (G). This shows that AT-rich DNA has a significant role in increasing the fluorescence and binding capabilities with other materials; Likewise, Amirbekyan et al. [71] viewed that the hydrogen bonding interference across the inner gapping amine groups of Hoechst derivative had more affinity and selectivity for DNA strands rich in A-T base, hence more intensified fluorescence.

As per the studies made by Zhao [72], the self-assemblage of the double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) upon the graphene layer is achieved by π-π stacking between the hydrophobic base pairs of DNA strands and the graphitic carbon rings, this association was intense for the DNA strands rich in AT-base pairs. It could be demonstrated that intensified fluorescence tests via tagging of DNA with the fluorescent C-dots in place of using harmful staining dyes would be more significant and sustainable for diagnostic applications. Integrating the C-dots with various metallic nanoparticles, dyes, microfluidics, fluorescein amides (FAM), or electrolytes can increase the number of potential biosensors. An illustrative diagram has been presented to describe the use of C-dots for analytical applications in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Integration of C-dots with various molecules for nucleic acid biosensing

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots can be designed to quench the fluorescence of certain dyes or vice-versa, in the presence of nucleic acids. This quenching can occur through various mechanisms, such as electron transfer or energy transfer [73]. When the nucleic acids bind to the surface of carbon dots, they may alter the fluorescence properties of the dyes, leading to a measurable change in fluorescence intensity. This change can be used as a signal for nucleic acid detection. Carbon dots can also enhance the fluorescence of dyes when they come into close proximity [74]. This enhancement can result from the surface plasmon resonance properties of carbon dots or other interactions between the carbon dots and the dye molecules [75]. Several examples of dyes used in conjunction with C-dots to devise DNA biosensing techniques.

Methylene blue is a thiazine dye of organic chloride consisting of methylthioninium and chloride ions. It has been commonly used as a colouring dye, antimicrobial and medication substance in the past few decades [76]. The use of methylene blue dye, a simple, sensitive and easy-to-use biosensor for the detection of circulating tumour DNA (ctDNA) was devised by Bai et al. [77] based on the fluorescence restoration approach. Methylene blue emanates methylthioninium anions, which interact with the negatively charged C-dots obtained from the lamp soot refluxed with oxidizing acid, to quench their fluorescence. When ctDNA is introduced to the MB-C-dots solution, methylthioninium ions are separated from the C-dots’ surface and adhere to the negatively charged chain of the template ctDNA, restoring the portion fluorescence intensity of the liberated C-dots. In this setup, the single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) could also restore the C-dot fluorescence. Moreover, due to poor binding between ssDNA and MB, its effectiveness was shown to be lower than that of dsDNA, the reason being that methylthioninium ions are bound to dsDNA via both electrostatic interaction and intercalation, but only electrostatic interaction with ssDNA. As a result, ds-DNA is more effectively removed from C-dots than ssDNA [78,79]. Further, the fluorescence intensity restoration can be quantified to get the sample load value by setting up a calibration curve, comprising a Stern-Volmer plot which was used for subsequent computation and qualification.

As a part of agarose gel base, ethidium bromide (EtBr), also known as homidium bromide, has been utilized extensively as a fluorescent dye in DNA tracking in agarose gel electrophoresis. Its luminous properties combined with C-dots have been used in the field of DNA biosensing [80]. Huang et al. [81] presented a biosensing technique designed with fluorescent C-dots and EtBr, an organic fluorescent dye, allowing a label-free and highly judicious detection of the sample dsDNA. In this technique, C-dots were utilized as standard fluorochromes, acting as a distinct recognizing element and a response signal in tandem with EtBr. Microwave irradiation was used to manufacture fluorescent C-dots, l-serine was used as the carbon source with polyethylene glycol and glycerin as the co-reactants. When EtBr was added to the C-dots solution, the electron transfer pathway effectively muted the fluorescence of the C-dots. In contrast, the fluorescence of EtBr was slightly amplified without involving fluorescence resonance energy transfer under the same excitation wavelength, as a result, the fluorescence colour changed from blue to deep red. Because EtBr and DNA have a powerful intercalating association, the photoluminescence intensity of EtBr increased significantly when the sample dsDNA was added, whereas the C-dots were almost impervious to DNA, resulting in a change in photoluminescence intensity as well as a distinguishable luminescence color which changed from dark to perky red, that can be interpreted visually [81].

Thiazole orange (TO) is an outstanding chiral cyanine dye, often implemented in fluorescence imaging and nucleic acid detection. The dye is practically non-fluorescent in buffer solutions, however, it exhibits a significant fluorescence amplification when linked with dsDNA [82]. On this basis, Jin et al. [83] reported a new and modest biosensing technology incorporating C-dots and TO nanoprobe (C-dot-TO) for ratiometric fluorescence detection of DNA. TO served as the identification and response unit in this approach, interacting with the target DNA for producing a strong turn-on emission at 530 nm whereas C-dots served as the reference unit due to their unchanging fluorescence. Preparation of amino-functionalized C-dots was done by heating a mixture of 2,2-(ethylene dioxy)bis(ethylamine) and citric acid. When the target DNA is unavailable, the nanoprobe emits solely C-dots’ bluish fluorescence while TO remains non-luminescent. When target DNA was introduced, a turn-on mechanism at the relevant wavelength developed, which steadily increased as the amount of the target or sample DNA increased. Keeping the luminescence of C-dots constant in the presence of several DNA samples, the absorption bands of C-dots-TO decreased significantly, and the ratio fluorescence spectral intensity increased, indicating the interaction of TO with DNA. The researchers used several ssDNA samples, but the dsDNA interaction was found to be most promising. The C-dots-TO nanoprobe was shown to have strong selectivity for the parallel G-quadruples (G-4) and dsDNA, over the antiparallel and ssDNA in their experimental finding [83].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of C-dots with metals and their derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots are extremely sensitive to the existence of metal ions in a solution, and several investigations have demonstrated that their luminescence changes (dampen or augment) in the presence of different metal ions [84]. There are two celebrated theories about the mechanics of C-dots’ surface electrons engagement with metallic ionic species. According to a few researchers, the quenching of fluorescence of C-dots brought about by the association of metal ions with the C-dots is purely static as seen in the ground state interaction between copper ions and C-dots to form nonluminous C-dot-metal ion complexes [85,86] whereas other findings show that surface electrons are prompt to engage in charge transfer dynamics, which might explain the strong interlinkage of C-dots with metal ions [87,88]. According to the research findings, the chemical interaction between ground-state or functionalized C-dots and metal ions produces stable C-dot-metal ion complexes (such as with Cu2+) [89]. Their emission quenching is expected to include a very efficient electron transfer towards metal ions, but the presence of other metal ionic species such as Zn2+ in a C-dot solution does not quench the luminescence qualities but somewhat improves the quantum yield [86]. This dynamic interplay between C-dots and metals, metal ions, metal nanoparticles [90], metal oxides and metal complexes offer a diverse range of strategies for enhancing sensitivity and selectivity in nucleic acid sensing. The interaction between C-dots and metal ions, phenomena like the exchange of electrons, Static quenching effect (SQE), Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), Inner Filler effect (IFE), Aggregate induced emission quenching (AIEQ), and Electron transfer mechanism (ETM) work upon [91]. These are all examples of C-dot sensing mechanisms based on photoluminescence alteration, shown in Fig. 2 . All of these routes, except for SQE are subjected to dynamic quenching [92]. The on-and-off attribute of C-dots fluorescence due to interaction with various molecules, ions and atoms make it available for the nucleic acid biosensors based on the fluorometric and colorimetric results, label-free sensing, multiplex detection and real-time monitoring. The Nucleic acid biosensors made by C-dots and their interaction with metal ions or their complexes have been discussed as follows.

Liu et al. [93] utilized terbium (Tb), a rare earth metal with atomic number 65 having +3 valency, in their circulating tumor ctDNA detection setup. They adopted a hydrothermal approach to manufacture Tb doped C-dots by using terbium, glucose, ethanol and ethylenediamine. These Tb-doped C-dots identified the ctDNA based on changes in the system's relative fluorescence when DNA is introduced to the Tb–C-dot solution, the negatively charged phosphate backbone of DNA interacts with the positively charged corners of Tb-doped C-dots, preventing the surface electron transitions for luminescence. The diminishing fluorescence with the increase in DNA concentration may therefore be employed for the quantification of the sample DNA/ctDNA introduced in their investigation, as seen in the quantified graph.

Genetic disorders, or mutation-induced diseases are a great threat to life, especially in the past few years with the increasing pollution, high radiation emitting devices usage, and unhealthy lifestyle. The aggregation of plausibly mutagenic abasic sites or lesions in DNA has been frequently suspected as a stress-inducing indicator for mutations, carcinogens, and malignancy [94]. The donation of electrons from C-dot surface functional groups to the copper ions has been studied in many types of research. In a copper ion and DNA solution, cupric ions undergo reduction, forming cuprous ions as intermediates and finally become elemental Cu, in this phenomenon nucleotides play a salient role in the formation of copper nanocomplexes (Cu-NC). This mechanism is hindered when abasic inclusions are present in DNA, as a result of which the solution is left with a high concentration of cupric ions [95]. On that basis, Singh et al. [96] posited a simple and economical fluorescence-based approach for detecting sites in dsDNA in which DNA was employed as a template for the creation of Cu-NC. The free cupric ions were quantified by the extent of quenching in the luminescence of the C-dots in solution, which reflects the existence of abasic sites in those DNA samples, as shown in Fig. 3 . Apart from fluorescence features, the binding of Cu–NCs to normal and abasic sites containing DNA differed significantly. In this biosensing methodology, hydrothermally produced C-dots from cysteamine and citric acid were implied. The quenching of the C-dots’ fluorescence is apparent and proportional to several abasic sites in the DNA.

Wang et al. [97] presented a DNA sensor constructed of N,N′-disuccinimidyl carbonate (DSC) that captures DNA by covalently trapping it on a diagnostic gadget based on a microfluidic paper (μPADs). Their technology combines easy covalent modification with signal enhancement to provide a robust chemiluminescence methodology for detecting DNA on inexpensive paper-based analytical instruments using a simple and rapid wax-screen printing technique. The μPADs provide a site for template DNA hybridization through a sandwich-type assay and the signal intensification label is done via C-dots stippled nano/microporous gold (C-dot@NPG). C-dots@NPG labeled signal is recorded on the DNA biosensor after the probe-template hybridization procedure; here, the signal was triggered by oxidant potassium permanganate which induces holes and electrons to trigger a radiant chemi-luminescence reaction of the C-dots@NPG.

Based on the quenching and restoration of C-dot fluorescence, a new and economical biosensor for recognizing a particular gene (HTLV1) of human T-lymphotropic virus type-1 was developed [98]. The adult T-cell leukaemia or lymphoma (ATL) is a kind of blood cancer caused by this virus. O-phenylenediamine and ethanol were utilized to make the C-dots employed in this biosensor, hydrothermally. The fluorescence of bright C-dots was quenched using ferric oxide nanoparticles coupled with gold nanoparticles (Fe3O4–Au). The C-dots had two probes covalently bonded to their surface which were complementary to HTLV1 gene. In the presence of a target, the coupled probes and target gene form a double helix structure that does not adsorb on the nanoparticle surface, allowing the C-dots fluorescence to remain unaffected, as illustrated in Fig. 4 , the sample load could be then calculated by determining the intensity or extent of the fluorescent C-dot luminescence [98].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical DNA biosensors based on C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: When compared with typical DNA sequencing methods, utilizing electrochemical biosensors to identify specific DNA sequences or DNA mutations can considerably shorten the assay time and simplify the analytical processes. These rapid monitoring techniques are required for prompt prevention and early diagnosis of infections or diseases. The C-dots have been shown to exhibit stronger electro-catalytic abilities than the other carbon-based nanomaterials, making them important participants in the advancement of electrochemical devices and techniques [99]. Electro-chemiluminescence (ECL) technology offers several advantages, including high sensitivity, minimal background signal, and a broad dynamic range, making it a suitable choice for biomarker detection. In this context, Liu et al., 2023 [100] presented a novel biosensor to detect small fragments of human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) DNA. The biosensor uses fluorine-nitrogen co-doped C-dots with robust and stable ECL properties. The incorporation of fluorine and nitrogen in the C-dots enhanced the intensity and stability of the ECL signal. To achieve susceptible detection, the researchers employed exonuclease III (Exo III)-assisted multiple recycling amplification to convert minute amounts of HIV-DNA fragments into a significant output of DNA. This output DNA subsequently triggers a horizontal hybridization chain reaction (H-HCR), forming a three-dimensional (3D) network DNA nanostructure, which immobilizes an ECL quencher (doxorubicin-ferrocenecarboxylic acid). The quencher effectively diminishes the ECL signal, which significantly improves the sensitivity of the proposed biosensor.
The quencher effectively diminishes the ECL signal [100].

The C-dots when made from graphene, graphene oxide derivatives, and various organic compounds of graphene, are known as Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which are minuscule fragments or specks of graphene with exceptional luminescent capabilities [101]. Ghalkhani et al. [102] have used carbon electrodes integrated with the Ag–Cs-GQD-NTiO2 based biosensor to detect Staphylococcus aureus specific gene. Therefore, both carbon nanostructures and graphene have been used in biosensing devices. Furthermore, Xiang et al. [103] developed a sophisticated electrochemical biosensor centered on the GQDs that substantially interacts with DNA. The probe DNA was intended to be complementary to Hepatitis B Virus (HBV) DNA; however, when the probe was made to be tightly coupled with the surface of GQDs modified electrodes in their setup, electron transport from the electrode to the electrochemically operative species potassium ferricyanide becomes impossible. Consequently, if the target HBV-DNA is discovered in the assay solution, the DNA probe will attach to it rather than the GQDs. As a result, the acquired peak currents of potassium ferricyanide ions would grow in varying degrees depending on the amount of the target HBV-DNA available. It gives a method that may be applied universally for DNA detection by redesigning with the other probes [103]. With excellent sensitivity and repeatability, this sensing device could identify complementary and mismatched nucleic acid sequences too.

Another electrochemical-based device was designed by Deng and collaborators [104], the DNA biosensor focused on the CQ-dots-functionalized graphene semiconductor field-effect transistors for a quite sensitive label-free DNA detection. It comprised a gate electrode upon which the C-dots were incapacitated through the mercaptoacetic acid-thiol group. ssDNA probes complementary to the target DNA were attached to already immobilized CQ-dots’ surface by strong electronic interactions. When a ssDNA template or target is provided, the ssDNA probe synergizes with it to create dsDNA and detaches from the surface of C-dots, generating a shift in the channel current response and Dirac voltage across the electrode. This biosensor is also capable of distinguishing one-base mismatched target DNA.

Another straightforward electrochemical label-free DNA biosensor was developed which were based on quantum C-dot manipulation employing neutral red (NR) as an electrochemical probe [105]. The surface trapped Au nanoparticles upon the electrode made up of indium tin oxide (Au-ITO) were used. This electrode was employed for electrochemical sensing of the Salmonella gene sequence. The bacterium is a deadly and well-known food poisoning pathogen. The surface of Au-ITO electrode was functionalized by immobilizing the mercapto groups (MER-Au-ITO) to attach probe aptasenser sequences (ssDNA). The redox indicator neutral red doped C-Dots (NR-CD) derived from a solvothermal method from glucose were used. When the NR-CD solution is introduced into the working electrode chamber, in the absence of target DNA the electronic transfer occurs between the mercapto groups and the NR-CDs, however, it is dramatically reduced when the probes connected to mercapto groups on the electrode surface hybridize with target DNA species, causing the voltammetric signals to change [105].

When it comes to viral detection, using optical or electrochemical approaches, the incorporation of C-dots provides several advantages as they have a changeable elemental constitution and opto-visual characteristics that may be altered, either by doping or passivation of C-dots. The C-dots can function both as electron recipients and donors [106]. Recognizing viruses by detecting their genetic material using C-dots in biosensing devices can boost the sensitivity and specificity of biosensing or diagnostic results. García-Mendiola et al. [107] used an electrochemical biosensor strategic plan to recognize the gene mutations by using unmodified oligonucleotide sequence probes, which differ from carbon nanotube (CNt) electrode-based biosensors. The CNt electrode-based biosensors are constrained to chemically modified oligonucleotides that cling to CNt's carboxyl groups through the 5′-amino group. As opposed to traditional gene assays, they employed screen-painted gold electrodes (CD-AuSPEs) modified by the C-dots produced from thermal carbonization of precursors: Ethyleneglycol bis-(2-aminoethylether)-N,N,N′,N′-tetraacetic acid (EGTA) and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (TRIS). The ssDNA/dsDNA probes were adsorbed on the CD-AuSPE modified electrodes, which could anneal with denatured (thermally treated) samples acquired from the post PCR process. The safranin stain was utilized as an indicator for the redox reaction produced by probe and template hybridization, yielding substantially distinct voltammetric outputs at the dsDNA and ssDNA-C-dots amended electrodes, the current signals for dsDNA were more extensive than those for ssDNA, the scheme exhibited in Fig. 5 . The current signal increases as the number of target sequences increases and vice versa, revealing mismatched pairing and, hence, mutations. As previously mentioned, this biosensor is also effective for analysing gene mutations in legitimate DNA isolated from human blood cells [107].

In the presence of tris(bipyridine)ruthenium ion [Ru(bpy)3]2+, C-dots can function as reactants in the anodic ECL of [Ru(bpy)3]2+. When a constant concentration of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ is applied, the effect of increasing C-dots’ concentration is more pronounced. The electrocatalytic process and electronic transaction occur, with the intensity of the [Ru(bpy)3]2+ oxidation peak increasing concurrently with the disappearance of the cathodic peak [108]. Because of the unique optical attributes of the [Ru(bpy)3]2+ ions coupled with the C-dots system to detect the DNA hybridization occurrences through ECL, an entirely novel biosensor to detect the pandemic devastation pathogen SARS-CoV-2 has been proposed. Guti'errez-Gálvez et al. [109] applied the [Ru(bpy)3]2+/C-dots framework in tandem with gold nanoparticles (AuNMs) to increase the electrochemical signal outputs to produce a discriminatory and precise SARS-CoV-2 biosensor. The Gold nanostructured electrodes were modified with a thiol-modified probe complementary to SARS-CoV-2 (SARS-CoV-2C) genomic section. A solution consisting of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ ions and C-dots were used to generate the electrochemical reaction, by executing a cyclic potential scan. Green synthesis of the C-dots was done from tiger nut milk via microwaving method. After immobilizing the DNA probes on the Au nanomaterial modified electrode, hybridization with the target occurs on the resulting biosensor surface which is detected in the electrochemical signal of the [Ru(bpy)3]2+/C-dots system and is enhanced by the involvement of the Au nanomaterials on the electrode surface. As a result, the [Ru(bpy)3]2+/C-dots electrochemical system can detect hybridization and the suggested biosensor works well, which is based on the difference in the electrochemical signal before and after hybridization. The biosensor uses human serum samples for diagnosing the pathogen's DNA. As reported by other authors previously, the reduction in electrochemical signal following the identification of certain sequences can be explained by the development of a DNA double helix which has a strong steric influence on the electron-transfer process of the [Ru(bpy)3]2+/C-dots system [110].

Pangajam et al. [111] performed polymerization of aniline (PANI) in the vicinity of C-dots/Zinc oxide (CDs-ZnO) employed as a customized electrode. Their technique was employed for supersensitive electrochemical detection of E. coli specific gene, under a remarkable electrical conductance of CDs-ZnO-PANI conductive layers. Aniline was polymerized by using the C-dots through a chemical oxidative polymerization technique to produce CDs-PANI, which was then integrated with a ZnO nanorod electrode (ZnO-CDs-PANI). PANI was used as a matrix for ZnO nanorods and C-dots diffusion to strengthen the electrode's selectivity and sensitivity. The C-dots were produced hydrothermally from honey and surface passivated by hydrogen peroxide. Finally, an amine-terminated capture probe ssDNA for the recognition of E. coli genome section was embedded on a CDs-ZnO-PANI modified electrode surface, which resulted in a reduction in peak current owing to the electron transfer obstruction. After immersing the electrode in the target DNA solution, the redox peaks fell even further, suggesting that the target DNA had effectively hybridized with the captured aptamer probes [111]. Even at low concentrations of ssDNA, the improved electrical conductivity of the ZnO-CDs-PANI modified electrode was revealed to be susceptible, impacting the reduction of peak current.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction with nucleotide sequences probes and C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Not shortly after the C-dots were noted, Sun et al. [6] underlined the important and extremely favourable influence of surface passivation. The surface passivation alters or enhances the capabilities of C-dots, as previously mentioned, but one particularly distinctive feature supplied after the passivation is the charge change of the C-dots surface. Because of the vast amount of carboxyl groups present, C-dots have a negative charge on their surface, but this may be changed into a positive charge by adding amine groups or specific organic polymeric chains. Using the binding agent poly-(propionylethlenimine-coethylenimine), Sun's team [6] previously demonstrated the creation of robust, bright water-soluble carbon nanotubes. The adherence of the polymer to the carbon nanostructures is dependent on the surface defect locations [112]. In addition, refluxing for a few hours with a high concentration of hot acids like nitric acid (2–3 N) produces essential surface defects with the incorporation of certain functional groups like carbonyl, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [113,114]. Despite the need for post-synthesis functionalization of C-dots, one-step synthesis with adequate precursors yields high-quality functionalized C-dots [115]. The surface of C-dots was passivated by Liu et al. [49] keeping three properties in consideration: first of all, optical detection for labelling substances via high fluorescence signals; secondly, the introduction of multivalence surface functional groups on the C-dots to make them interact with a wide range of target biomolecules while also improving aggregation properties; and thirdly, the addition of cationic properties to make the C-dots attract the negatively charged particles via electrostatic interactions. One advantage was that the C-dots coupled to the target DNA may be easily precipitated into bulk 3D volumes for visual detection experiments with less technical constraints. They created luminous C-dots out of the polyethyleneimine polymer which was highly cationic, and hence could interact electrostatically with the negatively charged phosphodiester chain of DNA.

Biosensing techniques via surface passivation of the C-dots have been put forward, Lee et al. [116] pioneered a direct and highly easy approach to DNA detection from the clinical isolates for the presence of Klebsiella pneumonia. In continuation of this, the cationic C-dots made by microwave-mediated thermal decomposition of polyethyleneimine and citric acid were applied. In the deionized water, the C-dots were allowed to interact with the sample DNA targets for 10 min. The presence of KPC-2 gene in bacterial DNA was the focus of the molecular targets, and the samples were DNA polymerase chain reaction products. The electrostatic association between cationic C-dots and the negatively charged target DNA samples dramatically reduced the fluorescence of C-dots after incubation. The interaction of the two disrupts the electronic transition in the surface functional group of C-dots, causing their photoluminescence to be quenched. The final result of this biosensing methodology was visually recognized by using a smartphone image. The above approach can be used in several nucleic acid biosensing applications where the endpoint detection is focused on DNA amplification products.

Khakbaz and Mahani [117] provided an effective platform based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) assay for tracking mir9-1 RNA gene, a breast cancer biomarker. C-dots were created using a one-step heating mechanism from citric acid and 2,2’-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine). Through π-π interactions, the FAM-labeled ssDNA complementary to the target biomarker miRNA was adsorbed on the C-dots surface, which prevented the C-dots from emitting fluorescence; this functioned as a sensing element in the study. Mir9-1 detection relied on the dampening of the C-dots' photoluminescence caused by FRET. In presence of the corresponding miRNA, FAM-labeled ssDNA linked to the surface of the C-dots hybridizes to create a dsDNA-miRNA helix. After hybridization the formed helix gets liberated from the surface of the C-dots, and the fluorescence strength is retrieved. The intensity of the restored luminescence was evaluated by using a fluorescence analyzer to determine the presence of the RNA target.

Researchers have been concerned about the Lower Limit of Detection (LOD) of Lab-on-a-chip devices. With the advent of C-dots with FRET mechanism and other breakouts in the field of such devices, this limitation seems to be fading. The radiationless passage of energy from a donor source to an acceptor target molecule is known as FRET. The energy is first absorbed by the giver molecule which could be a dye, chromophore, or ionic complex, and then is transferred to the acceptor molecule, which can induce color or fluorescence change [118]. Using this strategy, Qaddare and Salimi [119] together devised a technique by using C-dots embedded with ssDNA complementary to HIV-1 gene present in the HIV genome for detection of the Virus's RNA in the clinical samples. Hydrothermally synthesized C-dots from histidine were used. The complementary ssDNA probes were made covalently attached to C-dots’ surface to make C-dot-ssDNA (CD-ssDNA) complexes. These complexes were then adhered to the cysteamine coupled gold nanoparticles (AuNP-CD-ssDNA) which resulted in extinguishing the C-dots’ fluorescence by the phenomena of FRET. In presence of the template cDNA, received from reverse transcription of the sample genomes, CD-ssDNA having more affinity for the template cDNA get hybridized and the double helix leaves the surface of C-dots restoring their fluorescence. The recovered amount of fluorescence was directly proportionate to the sample load. To increase the sensitivity of this technique they applied graphene oxide modified AuNPs which showed promising results for increasing the LOD of the system.

Loo and co-workers [120] created an intriguing biosensor based on the adsorption of a fluorescent-labeled ssDNA (FAM-Lprobe) onto the surface of the C-dots, which results in quenching its fluorescence. In contrast, when FAM-Lprobe hybridizes with its complementary DNA target for generating dsDNA, the connections between dsDNA and C-dots weaken to the point where the newly formed dsDNA-C-dots complex dissociates, allowing FAM-Lprobe to retain its fluorescence. They also noted that in the absence of C-dots, FAM-Lprobe emits high fluorescence, therefore, for checking the efficacy, they used C-dots produced from two different sources: citric acid and malic acid. The interaction of the C-dots prepared by citric acid and malic acid causes the quenching of FAM-Lprobe fluorescence emission roughly by 23% and 39%, stating C-dots produced from malic acid adsorb FAM-Lprobes more efficiently [120]. It should be emphasized that C-dots were used as nanoquenchers in this project. The biosensor may also be used to differentiate between the single-base mismatch DNA from the complementary DNA targets by applying single nucleotide polymorphism analysis, which identifies variations in fluorescence intensity signals in response to the presence or absence of DNA targets. This can be used to detect mutations, gene presence or difference, species, and other modifications.

Freshly, Mohammadi et al. [121] have created a microRNA-21 biosensor with a dual fluorescence mechanism by combining two distinct C-dots, which were created by using a hydrothermal procedure with a blend of O-phenylenediamine (OPD) and 2-aminoterepthalic acid for producing blue luminescence C-dots (BCD), and solely OPD to produce yellow luminescence C-dots (YCD). They paired YCDs with BCDs which were covalently linked with an NH2-modified DNA probe that was complementary to the microRNA-21 target. This combination of B-YCDs exited at 360 nm exhibits dual fluorescence of 410 nm and 540 nm with the ratio of former emitted wavelength light more than the latter. This combination of B-YCDs exiting at 360 nm exhibits a dual fluorescence of 410 nm and 540 nm, with the former wavelength emitting more light than the latter. The fluorescence strength reduced at 410 nm while increased at 540 nm when the microRNA-21 sample was added; this dual combo of two discrete fluorescence was used as signals for the target screening and quantification. Finally, the bio-sensing technology was cast on a microfluidic device which was built of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) and glass that employs blood serum and MCF cells as samples, Fig. 6 [121]. The technique may also discriminate single-base mismatched microRNAs based on the signal intensity, making it helpful for determining various analytes to suit clinical demands in the diagnosis of disease-related microRNA.

A biosensor capable of detecting human papillomavirus (HPV) DNA in urine and cervical sample swabs was designed [122]. For this, the C-dots were hydrothermally produced using sunflower seed shells as a precursor, then doped with l-arginine and polyethyleneimine (PEI) to enhance their fluorescence properties. The DNA probe was created by modifying ssDNA with the fluorescence quenching agent 4-(4-dimethylaminobenzazo)benzoyl (Dabcyl). Through electrostatic interactions and FRET mechanism, the DNA probe induced fluorescence quenching within the C-dots. The ingenious design hinged on the strict base complementation pairing rule governing the binding of HPV 16 to the DNA probe. This interaction led to the separation of the DNA probe from the C-dots, triggering the activation of the C-dot's fluorescence (Fig. 7 ). Notably, this biosensor offered a straightforward approach in obtaining fluorescence-based results. Its innovative design and implementation signify significant advancements in the field of HPV detection and fluorescence-based biosensing techniques [122].

Recently, a fluorescent biosensor has been devised to detect E. coli in food samples. The biosensor's design uses ssDNA combined with C-dots [123]. The C-dots were synthesized using a super-hydrothermal method, wherein carrot juice was used as the carbon source. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots decreased upon attachment of ssDNA to their surface. However, an intriguing phenomenon was observed in the presence of E. coli. The ssDNA preferred binding to the bacteria through hydrogen bonding, increasing the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots. This unique response served as the basis for the biosensor's ability to detect E. coli [123].

Zainuddin et al. [124] detected Leptospira DNA by utilizing C-dots that were functionalized onto a tapered optical fiber. In this study, the C-dots were modified with complementary DNA probes to specifically recognize Leptospira gene. These probes hybridized with prepared complementary DNA samples to enable detection. When light passed through the optical fibre, it traversed the optical region where the probe and complementary DNA hybridization occurred. This hybridization caused a wavelength shift in the transmitted light due to surface modification of the C-dots, leading to signal generation. The generated signals were digitally recognized for outputting and quantifying the results. Additionally, the biosensor system could also distinguish between different samples by detecting the wavelength shifts. This breakthrough paves the way for future advancements in direct detection of specific nucleic acid sequences by combining optics, digital technology, and C-dots. All the above mentioned biosensing techniques with their linear range of detection, target and interacting component with C-dots, have been summarized in Table 1 .

Biosensors are being developed to reduce the cost, time and arrangements of the diagnostic setup. Altogether, the C-dot based sensing systems are compatible with and devoid of the obstacles encountered in the current Gold-standard procedures such as RT-PCR. The learning from the COVID-19 was that the POC devices are urgently required for preventing and checking the spread of the virus. Understanding the intrinsic immune recognition of the pathogen-derived nucleic acids has made tremendous strides in recent years, as the need for food safety and medical emergencies induced by infections has spurted. Furthermore, the structural data from various genetic material sensors have undoubtedly provided necessary knowledge regarding the actual events that these sensors depend upon. The inexpensive manufacture, simple availability, ecologically benign, electrical/optical qualities, strong biocompatibility and wide substrate scope are primarily connected with the economics of the study and implementation. These are the leading causes of explosion in C-dots based biosensor development and usage in varied sectors. Regardless, carbon is a remarkable element in terms of its chemical properties which allow it to exist in such a diversified form in nature, including the composition of biological and the constructed world. Carbon, whether in its nanocomplexes, or organic molecules, is the most common material for use in almost every possible field. Apart from the numerous allotropes, the introduction of C-dots has opened up a unique line of photoluminescence and electron transition in front of scientists, which led these substances to be peculiarly studied and dominate in various fields of applications. Still, the advancement and knowledge regarding the mechanisms of their interaction with other molecules or nanocomplexes are limited. In the current era of discovery and confirmation, the genetic material (DNA/RNA) based identification array is the only way considered to be concrete in the living world for empirical acceptance.

According to the published studies regarding nucleic acid biosensing, C-dots have worked as bridging the gap between template identification and interpretation of the result by synergistic actions with different nanotools. For the recognition or identification of DNA/RNA, a complementary probe to hybridize a specific site at the target is required. The C-dots have an affinity towards nucleic acid, which can be altered in the presence of complementary target or other additives, because of which they have been employed in the form of aptamer attached probe-complexes. Their luminescence and wavelength shifting attributes have been used to depict visual endpoint results. Simultaneously, their semi-conductance and electron transition characters for electrochemical and voltammetric result explications. While comparing different types of C-dot based biosensors, the cost, time, accuracy and limit of detection become a topic of discussion, analysis and research. The fluorometric biosensors are cheaper and handy than the other types; but on the basis of accuracy, LOD and speedy results electrochemical biosensors are more prominent; plus the former types are better for end-point detection and the latter for real-time detection techniques. Additionally, few electrometric biosensors have been designed explicitly considering the recognition of mutations or genetic modifications via mismatched base pairs detection. In these techniques as the number of mismatched pairs or strands increases, the current signal alters in proportion. The electrochemical biosensing systems have been designed to perform better due to their growing need for precise and discriminating single or multiplexed detection of analytes at a molecular level which seems to be fulfilled elegantly with the incorporation of C-dots or GQDs in the last few years, as per the available literature. Also, the C-dots have been used to modify the electrodes in an electrochemical system to stimulate the signal conductance or output, which has replaced graphene and GO in this manner [125]. The use of naturally available substances including bio-wastes for manufacturing C-dots is a significant bonus in their commendation. Talking about the lack of phases and gaps in the area of biosensing using C-dots, notwithstanding the benefits highlighted above, many more investigations have to be done regarding such detection or identification of the systems integrating C-dots which are still in their infancy. The lack of comprehensive and deployable procedure to manufacture high-quality C-dots with preferred structures, which now deal with no uniform synthetic routes, including site for contaminants, might be one of the causes of such delays, making a modest headway in this field. One of the primary debates is over the fluorescence mechanism dealing with its true origin and connections with the conditions under which precursors react to form C-dots. Even so, when created by using identical precursors and manufacturing approaches, C-dots from various research teams have exhibited differing features. This has resulted in a hindrance to detection capabilities and outcomes, restricting the commercial application of such biosensors [41,126]. Also, the C-dots interaction with chemicals such as dyes and metallic salts predominantly quenches their fluorescence, supporting the electron transition theory of PL. However, metals such as zinc have been reported to stimulate fluorescence and fluorophores; which must be meticulously explored to disclose the proper key mechanism of the interplay of C-dots with other compatible tools. The high unpredictability and intricacy of C-dots’ structure could be resolved by integrating conceptual research with the experimental construction of structural models [127,128].

The key characteristic of the C-dots that gives them the target interaction properties is their ease of surface modification and passivation which is caused by the superficial presence of diverse functional groups. On the other hand, these groups may interact with some substances to produce false signals causing significant obstruction. To address such concerns, a strong emphasis on the manufacturing process, pathway, and outcome of C-dots would be a substantial step in understanding their creation, prediction of the formed structure and behaviour, after which they can be used for a variety of purposes. Secondly, the incorporation of selected functional groups and sophisticated passivation catching or sensing the real target is required. The morphology of C-dots and their electron exchange mechanisms shall be uprooted deep to establish a clear relation between their electrochemical and photoluminescence properties. Furthermore, the C-dots have been discovered to have many additional qualities, such as catalysis or electrode materialism, which need to be explored further in their arena where they may serve beyond. Anti-interference of the unwanted targets or results has to be brought up to produce high quality results with accuracy in miniature devices. As the research in field of wavelength shift analysis is speeding up, the colorimetric or fluorometric based C-dots biosensors can pioneer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantages and disadvantages of use of C-dots a probe
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots exhibit high fluorescence quantum yield, enabling excellent sensitivity for detecting analytes, including biomolecules like DNA. Gold nanoparticles and quantum dots may offer comparable sensitivity levels, but it depends on the specific material and design. Quantum dots, for instance, can also provide high sensitivity due to their unique optical properties. C-dots are generally biocompatible and non-toxic, making them suitable for in vivo and biological applications. Some metal nanoparticles, like gold nanoparticles, can also be biocompatible, but their surface functionalization is critical to ensure biocompatibility. C-dots are relatively cost-effective due to their simple synthesis process and the abundance of carbon precursors. Technologies based on gold nanoparticles and quantum dots, can be more expensive to produce or purchase.

C-dots' stability can vary depending on the synthesis method and surface functionalization. They may exhibit limited stability under harsh conditions. However, gold nanoparticles, can be stable under certain conditions but may also undergo aggregation or degradation in certain environments.

The specificity of carbon dots in biosensing applications depends on the functionalization and target recognition strategies. Non-specific interactions may affect specificity. Enzyme-based sensors can offer high specificity, especially when coupled with appropriate recognition elements, like enzymes or antibodies. C-dots can be synthesized with different emission wavelengths, enabling multiplexing, but their spectral range might be limited compared to other technologies. Quantum dots and microarrays can provide a broader spectral range, allowing for higher levels of multiplexed detection. C-dots can offer a good signal-to-noise ratio due to their high fluorescence intensity, but background signals from non-specific interactions should be considered. However, Surface plasmon resonance can provide high signal-to-noise ratios, particularly in label-free biosensing applications.

C-dots typically require fluorescence-based readout, which can be achieved using standard fluorescence spectroscopy instruments. Surface-enhanced Raman scattering, require specialized instrumentation but can offer unique advantages, such as label-free detection and single-molecule sensitivity. C-dots exhibit with unique properties that make them suitable as probes for various applications, including DNA detection. C-dots are generally considered biocompatible and non-toxic, making them safe for DNA detection in living cells or organisms. C-dots can be easily synthesized with controllable sizes, allowing for tuning their optical and electronic properties. This feature enables customization for specific DNA detection applications and enhances sensitivity and selectivity. C-dots show good photostability, meaning they do not readily degrade or photobleach under prolonged light exposure. This property ensures reliable and long-lasting fluorescence signals for DNA detection experiments. C-dots exhibit a high quantum yield, which means they efficiently emit fluorescent signals when excited with light. This high fluorescence intensity enhances the sensitivity of DNA detection, enabling the detection of low DNA concentrations. The synthesis of C-dots is relatively straightforward and can be done using a variety of precursors and methods. This ease of synthesis makes them cost-effective and accessible for various research and diagnostic purposes. However, they have a few disadvantages, C-dots may suffer from limited long-term stability, especially under harsh environmental conditions or in biological media. This limitation may affect the reliability of DNA detection over extended periods. In some cases, C-dots can tend to aggregate, leading to quenching of fluorescence signals or altered optical properties. C-dots may interact non-specifically with biomolecules, including DNA, leading to false-positive signals or reduced specificity in DNA detection assays. Careful design and functionalization are required to minimize such interactions. The fluorescence emission of C-dots may be limited to certain wavelengths, which can affect multiplexing or the ability to simultaneously detect multiple DNA targets with distinct emission wavelengths.

This review article summarises the application of the C-dots for nucleic acid biosensing techniques, the C-dots interact with various substances and chemicals to generate the required pathway results such as electrometric, electrochemical, colorimetric, fluorometric etc. Based on such budding technologies, the C-dots can serve as a convenient medium in dipsticks or biosensing chips which will fill the gap of the utmost required short-time assays and definite nucleic acid detection devices. The bio-environment continuously expresses itself in varied manifestations, adding layers to the system's complexity in which we must periodically be prepared to cope with its new changed forms and unforeseen phenomena. Thus, the research gap in utilizing the rapid DNA detection methods, beyond RT-PCR-based tests, can be effectively addresses by the biosensors discussed in this article. These biosensors, when integrated with a simple and straightforward technology, have the potential to revolutionize the future market landscape. By bridging this gap, advancements in DNA detection can be achieved, offering faster and more efficient diagnostic solutions. Whatever conundrum remains to be revealed about the use of C-dots in biosensing devices, with pioneering advances in science, technology, and research, these devices will soon take off to replace the existing techniques for genetic material detection.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
